questions.question_text: A bill for ₹ 6000 is drawn on July 14 at 5 months. It is discounted on 5th October at 10%. Find the banker’s discount, true discount, banker’s gain and the money that the holder of the bill receives.
questions.solution:
Face value of the bill = ₹ 6000.
Date on which the bill was drawn = July 14 at 5 months.
Nominally due date = December 14. Legally due date = December 17.
Date on which the bill was discounted = October 5.
Unexpired time : Oct. Nov. Dec.
26 + 30 + 17 = 73 days = $\frac{1}{5}$ year.
$\therefore$ B.D. = S.I. on ₹ 6000 for $\frac{1}{5}$ year = $\left(6000\times10\times\frac{1}{5}\times\frac{1}{100}\right)$ = ₹ 120.
T.D. = $\left[\frac{6000\times10\times\frac{1}{5}}{100+\left(10\times\frac{1}{5}\right)}\right] = \left(\frac{12000}{102}\right)$ = ₹ 117.64.
$\therefore$ B.G. = (B.D.) – (T.D.) = ₹ (120 – 117.64) = ₹ 2.36.
Money received by the holder of the bill = ₹ (6000 – 120) = ₹ 5880.

questions.question_text: If the true discount on a certain sum due 6 months hence at 15% is ₹ 120, what is the banker’s discount on the same sum for the same time and at the same rate?
The banker’s discount on ₹ 1800 at 12% per annum is equal to the true discount on ₹ 1872 for the same time at the same rate. Find the time.
questions.solution:
S.I. on ₹ 1800 = T.D. on ₹ 1872.
$\therefore$ P.W. of ₹ 1872 is ₹ 1800.
$\therefore$ ₹ 72 is S.I. on ₹ 1800 at 12%.
$\therefore$ Time = $\left(\frac{100\times72}{12\times1800}\right)$ year = $\frac{1}{3}$ year = 4 months.

questions.question_text: The banker’s discount and the true discount on a sum of money due 8 months hence are ₹ 120 and ₹ 110 respectively. Find the sum and the rate percent.
questions.solution:
Sum = $\left(\frac{B.D.\times T.D.}{B.D. - T.D.}\right) = \left(\frac{120\times110}{120 - 110}\right)$ = ₹ 1320.
Since B.D. is S.I. on sum due, so S.I. on ₹ 1320 for 8 months is ₹ 120.
$\therefore$ Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times120}{1320\times\frac{2}{3}}\right)$% = $13\frac{7}{11}$%.

questions.question_text: The present worth of a bill due sometime hence is ₹ 1100 and the true discount on the bill is ₹ 110. Find the banker’s discount and the banker’s gain.
The banker’s discount on ₹ 1650 due a certain time hence is ₹ 165. Find the true discount and the banker’s gain.
questions.solution:
Sum = $\frac{B.D.\times T.D.}{B.D. - T.D.} = \frac{B.D.\times T.D.}{B.G.}$
$\therefore$ $\frac{T.D.}{B.G.} = \frac{Sum}{B.D.} = \frac{1650}{165} = \frac{10}{1}$
Thus, if B.G. is Re 1, T.D. = ₹ 10.
If B.D. is ₹ 11, T.D. = ₹ 10. If B.D. is ₹ 165, T.D. = $\left(\frac{10}{11}\times165\right)$ = ₹ 150.
And, B.G. = ₹ (165 – 150) = ₹ 15.

questions.question_text: What rate percent does a man get for his money when in discounting a bill due 10 months hence, he deducts 10% of the amount of the bill?
questions.solution:
Let, amount of the bill = ₹ 100. Money deducted = ₹ 10.
Money received by the holder of the bill = ₹ (100 – 10) = ₹ 90.
$\therefore$ S.I. on ₹ 90 for 10 months = ₹ 10.
$\therefore$ Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times10}{90\times\frac{10}{12}}\right)$% = $13\frac{1}{3}$%.

The banker’s gain on a bill due 1 year hence at 12% per annum is ₹ 6. The true discount is
The banker’s discount on a bill due 4 months hence at 15% is ₹ 420. The true discount is
The banker’s gain on a sum due 3 years hence at 12% per annum is ₹ 270. The banker’s discount is
The present worth of a sum due sometime hence is ₹ 576 and the banker’s gain is ₹ 16. The true discount is
The banker’s discount on ₹ 1600 at 15% per annum is the same as true discount on ₹ 1680 for the same time and at the same rate. The time is
questions.solution:
S.I. on ₹ $1600$ = T.D. on ₹ $1680$.
$\therefore$ ₹ $1600$ is the P.W. of ₹ $1680$, i.e.,
₹ $80$ is S.I. on ₹ $1600$ at $15\%$.
$\therefore$ Time = $\left(\frac{100\times80}{1600\times15}\right)$ year = $\frac{1}{3}$ year = $4$ months.

questions.question_text: The banker’s discount on a sum of money for $1\frac{1}{2}$ years is ₹ 558 and the true discount on the same sum for 2 years is ₹ 600. The rate percent is
questions.solution:
B.D. for $\frac{3}{2}$ years = ₹ $558$. B.D. for $2$ years
= ₹ $\left(558\times\frac{2}{3}\times2\right)$ = ₹ $744$.
T.D. for $2$ years = ₹ $600$.
$\therefore$ Sum = ₹ $\left(\frac{B.D.\times T.D.}{B.D.-T.D.}\right)$ = ₹ $\left(\frac{744\times600}{144}\right)$ = ₹ $3100$.
Thus, ₹ $744$ is S.I. on ₹ $3100$ for $2$ years.
$\therefore$ Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times744}{3100\times2}\right)\% = 12\%$.

questions.question_text: The banker’s discount of a certain sum of money is ₹ 72 and the true discount on the same sum for the same time is ₹ 60. The sum due is
The banker’s discount on a certain sum due 2 years hence is $\frac{11}{10}$ of the true discount. The rate percent is
questions.solution:
Let T.D. be ₹ $1$. Then, B.D. = $\frac{11}{10}$ ₹ = ₹ $1.10$.
$\therefore$ Sum = ₹ $\left(\frac{1.10\times1}{1.10-1}\right)$ = ₹ $\left(\frac{110}{10}\right)$ = ₹ $11$.
$\therefore$ S.I. on ₹ $11$ for $2$ years is ₹ $1.10$.
$\therefore$ Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times1.10}{11\times2}\right)\% = 5\%$.

questions.question_text: The banker’s gain on a certain sum due $1\frac{1}{2}$ years hence is $\frac{3}{25}$ of the banker’s discount. The rate percent is :
questions.solution:
Let, B.D. = ₹ $1$. Then, B.G. = ₹ $\frac{3}{25}$.
$\therefore$ T.D. = (B.D. - B.G.) = ₹ $\left(1-\frac{3}{25}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{22}{25}$.
Sum = ₹ $\left(\frac{1\times\frac{22}{25}}{1-\frac{22}{25}}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{22}{3}$.
S.I. on ₹ $\frac{22}{3}$ for $1\frac{1}{2}$ years is ₹ $1$.
$\therefore$ Rate = $\left(\frac{100\times1}{\frac{22}{3}\times\frac{3}{2}}\right)\% = 9\frac{1}{9}\%$.
